perturbation.original_text: DISCLOSEE shall give NUSD reasonable notice prior to such disclosure and shall comply with any applicable protective order or equivalent issued on behalf of NUSD.
perturbation.changed_text: DISCLOSEE shall give NUSD 24-hour notice prior to such disclosure and shall comply with any applicable protective order or equivalent issued on behalf of NUSD.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the notice period to '24-hour notice' may be insufficient under certain circumstances, especially if the disclosure involves sensitive student information governed by FERPA, potentially hindering the school district's ability to respond appropriately and safeguard student data. A reasonable period of notice depends on the specific circumstances and may be longer than 24 hours.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act (FERPA)
perturbation.law_explanation: FERPA requires educational agencies and institutions to protect the privacy of student education records. While FERPA does not specify an exact timeframe for notice, a 24-hour notice period before disclosing confidential information pursuant to a judicial order may not provide NUSD with enough time to adequately protect student privacy rights, especially if additional legal action or parental notification is required. The contradiction lies in potentially limiting NUSD's ability to comply with FERPA's requirements for safeguarding student records by imposing an unreasonably short notice period.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a potential contradiction by reducing the notice period to a fixed 24 hours. The Law Explanation states that 'a 24-hour notice period before disclosing confidential information pursuant to a judicial order may not provide NUSD with enough time to adequately protect student privacy rights.' This suggests that the fixed timeframe may be insufficient to comply with FERPA, which requires protecting student education records. Snippet 1 indicates that FERPA outlines conditions when prior consent is not required to disclose information, and the 'reasonable notice' in the original contract suggests flexibility to meet these conditions, but the new text creates an inconsistency regarding these time-sensitive obligations in a contract that violates federal or state law. Therefore, the modified contract could be deemed unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Documents prepared by DISCLOSEE using Confidential Information, or derived therefrom, shall be destroyed upon request of NUSD.
perturbation.changed_text: Documents prepared by DISCLOSEE using Confidential Information, or derived therefrom, shall be destroyed within 180 days of request of NUSD.
perturbation.explanation: Specifying a 180-day timeframe for the destruction of documents containing confidential information contradicts the requirement for prompt action, especially in cases involving student records, where immediate destruction might be necessary to prevent unauthorized disclosure.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Education Code Section 49070 grants parents the right to access and challenge the content of their child's student records, and requires educational agencies to respond promptly to such requests. A 180 day delay in destroying documents could prevent compliance with parental requests. The contradiction arises because the extended timeframe undermines the intent of the law to ensure timely access and correction of student records, creating a risk of non-compliance with deadlines and response requirements.

ELEMENTARY AND SECONDARY EDUCATION [33000 - 65001] ( Title 2 enacted by Stats. 1976, Ch. 1010. ) DIVISION 4. INSTRUCTION AND SERVICES [46000 - 65001] ( Division 4 enacted by Stats. 1976, Ch. 1010. ) PART 27. PUPILS [48000 - 49703] ( Part 27 enacted by Stats. 1976, Ch. 1010. ) CHAPTER 6.5. Pupil Records [49060 - 49085] ( Chapter 6.5 enacted by Stats. 1976, Ch. 1010. ) ARTICLE 4. Rights of Parents [49070 - 49072] ( Article 4 enacted by Stats. 1976, Ch. 1010. ) 49070. Following an inspection and review of a pupil’s records, the parent or guardian of a pupil or former pupil of a school district may challenge the content of any pupil record. (a) The parent or guardian of a pupil may file a written request with the superintendent of the school district to correct or remove any information recorded in the pupil’s written records that the parent or guardian alleges to be any of the following: (1) Inaccurate. (2) An unsubstantiated personal conclusion or inference. (3) A conclusion or inference outside of the observer’s area of competence. (4) Not based on the personal observation of a named person with the time and place of the observation noted. (5) Misleading. (6) In violation of the privacy or other rights of the pupil. (b) (1) Within 30 day
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 180-day timeframe for destroying confidential student records, which contradicts the requirement for prompt action to comply with parental requests under California Education Code Section 49070. The original text stipulates that documents should be destroyed 'upon request', implying immediate action. The changed text weakens this obligation by allowing a delay of up to 180 days. As stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1: 'Following an inspection and review of a pupil’s records, the parent or guardian of a pupil or former pupil of a school district may challenge the content of any pupil record.' This implies a need for accessible and accurate records which might be compromised by a lengthy delay in document destruction.

perturbation.original_text: DISCLOSEE shall assist NUSD in the notification of students and parents/guardians in the event of unauthorized disclosure of pupil records.
perturbation.changed_text: DISCLOSEE may assist NUSD in the notification of students and parents/guardians in the event of unauthorized disclosure of pupil records, at DISCLOSEE's sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall' to 'may, at DISCLOSEE's sole discretion' makes the assistance optional. This conflicts with regulations that place a duty on schools to notify parents/guardians of breaches.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Education Code Section 49079.8 mandates that school districts must notify parents/guardians in the event of a data breach involving student information. By making DISCLOSEE's assistance in this notification discretionary, the modified text undermines the district's ability to promptly and effectively fulfill its legal obligation to inform parents of breaches, potentially violating the notification requirements outlined in the statute. The change from 'shall' to 'may' introduces the risk of non-compliance with the mandatory notification provision.

perturbation.justification: The change from "shall" to "may, at DISCLOSEE's sole discretion" directly contradicts the legal obligation for notification in case of a data breach, as explained in the Law Explanation. The original text imposed a mandatory duty, while the changed text makes assistance optional. The Perturbation Explanation confirms that this undermines the school district's ability to fulfill its legal obligation. The Law Explanation references California Education Code Section 49079.8, which mandates notification, and the scraped snippet mentions EDC 49079.8, supporting the claim that the modification creates a potential violation of the notification requirements. This falls under the Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction definition, where a deadline or requirement violates federal or state law.

perturbation.original_text: If either NUSD or DISCLOSEE employs attorneys to enforce any rights arising out of or relating to this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover reasonable attorneys’ fees.
perturbation.changed_text: If either NUSD or DISCLOSEE employs attorneys to enforce any rights arising out of or relating to this Agreement, neither party shall be entitled to recover attorneys’ fees, regardless of who prevails.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the provision for the prevailing party to recover attorney's fees can conflict with California statutes that provide for attorney's fees in certain types of cases, such as those involving violations of privacy or civil rights.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1021.5
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1021.5 allows for an award of attorney's fees to a successful party in certain cases that result in the enforcement of an important right affecting the public interest. If a dispute under the NDA involved such a right (e.g., related to student privacy), the contractual provision denying attorney's fees to the prevailing party would conflict with the court's power to award fees under CCP 1021.5. The blanket denial of attorney's fees could therefore undermine the enforcement of important public rights protected by California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1021.5 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 6. Of Costs [1021 - 1038] ( Chapter 6 enacted 1872. ) 1021.5. Upon motion, a court may award attorneys’ fees to a successful party against one or more opposing parties in any action which has resulted in the enforcement of an important right affecting the public interest if: (a) a significant benefit, whether pecuniary or nonpecuniary, has been conferred on the general public or a large class of persons, (b) the necessity and financial burden of private enforcement, or of enforcement by one public entity against another public entity, are such as to make the award appropriate, and (c) such fees should not in the interest of justice be paid out of the recovery, if any. With respect to actions involving public entities, this section applies to allowances against, but not in favor of, public entities, and no claim shall be required to be filed therefor, unless one or more successful parties and one or more opposing parties are public entities, in which case no claim shall be required to be filed therefor under Part 3 (commencing with Section 900) of Division 3.6 of Title 1 of the Governm
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: CHAPTER 6. OF COSTS - Sections 1021-1038 :: California Code of Civil Procedure :: 2005 California Code :: California Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the California Code 2024 California Code 2023 California Code 2022 California Code 2005 California Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2005 California Code of Civil Procedure Sections 1021-1038 CHAPTER 6. OF COSTS CODE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE SECTION 1021-1038 1021. Except as attorney's fees are specifically provided for by statute, the measure and mode of compensation of attorneys and counselors at law is left to the agreement, express or implied, of the parties; but parties to actions or proceedings are entitled to their costs, as hereinafter provided. 1021.4. In an action for damages against a defendant based upon that defendant's commission of a felony offense for which that defendant has been convicted, the court may, upon motion, award reasonable attorney's fees to a prevailing plaintiff against the defendant who has been convicted of the felony. 1021.5. Upon motion, a court may award attorneys' fees to a successful party against one or more opposing parties in any action which has resulted in the enforcement of an important right affecting the public interest if: (a) a significant benefit, whether pecuniary or nonpecuniary, has been conferred on the general public or a large class of persons, (b) the necessity and financial burden of private enforcement, or of enforcement by one public entity against another public entity, are such as to make the award appropriate, and (c) such fees should not in the interest of justice be paid out of the recovery, if any. With respect to actions involving public entities, this section applies to allowances against, but not in favor of, public entities, and no claim shall be required to be filed therefor, unless one or more successful parties and one or more opposing parties are public entities, in which case no claim shall be required to be filed therefor under Part 3 (commencing with Section 900) of Division 3.6 of Title 1 of the Government Code. Attorneys' fees awarded to a public entity pursuant to this section shall not be increased or decreased by a multiplier based upon extrinsic circumstances, as discussed in Serrano v. Priest, 20 Cal. 3d 25, 49. 1021.6. Upon motion, a court after reviewing the evidence in the principal case may award attorney's fees to a person who prevails on a claim for implied indemnity if the court finds (a) that the indemnitee through the tort of the indemnitor has been required to act in the protection of the indemnitee's interest by bringing an action against or defending an action by a third person and (b) if that indemnitor was properly notified of the demand to bring the action or provide the defense and did not avail itself of the opportunity to do so, and (c) that the trier of fact determined that the indemnitee was without fault in the principal case which is the basis for the action in indemnity or that the indemnitee had a final judgment e
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the provision for the prevailing party to recover attorney's fees, regardless of who prevails. This directly contradicts California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1021.5, as highlighted in both scraped snippets. CCP 1021.5 states that a court "may award attorneys’ fees to a successful party against one or more opposing parties in any action which has resulted in the enforcement of an important right affecting the public interest". The changed text eliminates this possibility, creating a legal contradiction, because it could undermine the court's power to award fees in cases involving public interest, directly conflicting with the law.
